segs.utf8:
Can we really end all new cases
of HIV globally by 2030?

It's a goal that's been set by
the United Nations,

but with millions
still affected by the disease

and progress being threatened by cuts
to USAID funding.

How close are we
to making it a reality?

That's what we're going to be talking
about today on What in the World?

from the BBC World Service
I'm Hannah Gelbart.

And to unpack this a bit more, joining
us from our Lagos bureau in Nigeria
is BBC reporter Makuochi Okafor.

Hi. Welcome back to the podcast.
Hi, Hannah.

Can you start by just outlining
what the symptoms are of HIV?

Okay, so HIV is
a sexually transmitted disease.
It attacks a person's immune system.

Now the immune system is
what sort of protects the body

from getting infected
from other diseases.

What HIV does is
once it attacks the immune system,

the body is weak
and can no longer protect itself.

So other diseases,
you get infected easily

from things like malaria,
tuberculosis, and other infection.

Any infection that is sort of
in your locality or in your area.

What are some of
the main things that people believe

about HIV and Aids
that actually just aren't true?

There are many wrong information
people have out there, for example,

that HIV can easily be contacted through
physical contact like handshake or hugs.

These are wrong.
And these sort of mix, you know,

when people have HIV or, you know,
a family member that have it,

they don't quickly seek care because
they're trying to keep it hush hush

so people wouldn't run away from
their friends or their family members.
These are sort of one.

And there's also this
assumption that when you have HIV,

that means you're promiscuous or
you're keeping multiple sex partners.

There is also this wrong assumption
that HIV is a punishment from God.

You know, because people feel
like you're not moral enough

and that's why you're being punished.
These are sort of the few very popular

and prevalent misinformation
around HIV and Aids,
and HIV isn't a death sentence.

You know, thankfully we have lots
of tools to help people survive.
So, sometimes people think that

once they get HIV
all this means you're going to die.

But this isn't accurate because we've
seen lots of people live over decades,

you know, with available tools
and treatments around HIV.

So these are some
of the misinformation around HIV

that you can hear both here on the
continent and even beyond the continent.

As you say,
there is treatment for HIV so that it

can now no longer even be detectable
in people's blood. Right?

Yes, you're very accurate. So there
are drugs called antiretroviral drugs

which people living
with HIV can take.

Now this sees that when you're taking
this drug you do not pass

on the virus to,
for example, a partner.

Or there are drugs
that prevent mothers from passing
on HIV to their unborn kids.

There are also drugs like PrEP, which
sort of protects people who feel

like they might be getting
in contact

with HIV,
with the virus,

you know, more or less.
So there are lots of tools available.

And then there are things
like contraceptives that,

to an extent, can also protect you–
things like condoms

protect you from getting HIV or Aids.
So there are lots of tools now.

So it's not as scary, quote unquote,
as it was
you know, many years ago.

Where are cases of HIV highest
in the world right now?

Globally, the African region is
the region

with the highest HIV prevalence,
particularly South Africa,

and with about eight million people
living with HIV in the country.
Countries like Botswana and Malawi

in Africa have seen, you know,
considerable reduction,

but also in the continent on.
Speaking about continents,

the European continent
has seen slight increase recently.

But in Africa,
the Tigray region

in Ethiopia has seen an increase
in cases of HIV.

And this has been because there's
been prolonged conflict there.

And there's also cases of sexual
violence being used in this conflict

that has impacted lots
of people living there.

What about PrEP? How is
that contributing to changing rates?

You said that they've gone down quite
a lot in the last ten, 20 years.

This has been such a strong tool
in preventing

and protecting people
from getting HIV.
And what people have done

and what some countries
have done is include PrEP

in their primary healthcare services,
um, which has been very helpful and

lots of countries it's either
subsidised or is free.

So you also kind of increase
and improve access to getting it.

So, the medication has actually been a
strong tool in helping protect people

and prevent HIV
across countries globally.

Discrimination and stigma is
a huge problem for a lot of people.
I'll give you an instance. I know,

and I've heard people say that
once they see someone taking PrEP,

they assume the person is HIV
positive and this is not accurate.

And there's so much stigma tied to HIV.
So that means even those who do have HIV

or who should be taking PrEP
are discouraged from taking it

because they feel like, oh,
people are going to see me taking it,

or people are going to see me walking
to the hospital to pick up PrEP

and they wouldn't take it. These
are one of or some of the issues.

And then there is also this issue
where people think

that if you're taking PrEP, you are
associated with the LGBTQ community.

And this is very wrong because
PrEP is

for everyone who should be taking it
as prescribed.

And one of the issues,
of course, is going to be funding

at the start of the year,
the US president, Donald Trump,

said that he is dismantling USAID,
which is the US foreign aid.

What effect is that having
on HIV treatment on the continent.

For many countries,
for example, I remember the WHO

was saying that countries like Nigeria
or Kenya could run out of medication,

and that's because most
countries have relied on aid

in having these drugs available
for their citizens.

I'm not saying
that it's completely all the drugs,

but most of the drugs
that are being used,
they get support from foreign aid.

But now that you know that's been
cut, it's definitely going to impact
people's access to these drugs.

For example,
I know there's an NGO in Nigeria

that used to offer free PrEP
for citizens and for people.
Sometime in the wake of this cut,

they put out an announcement to say
they won't be doing that any more

because they no more have access
to bringing in these drugs.

And this means that people now have
to either rely on getting it

over the counter
and paying out of pocket.

For many people,
this would be too high.

I do know that the drugs,
for example,

in Nigeria go as high as 70,000 Naira
and that is a lot for many people.
There's data from South Africa saying

that there's been reduced testing,
in lots of communities.

And this has been
because both the testing

and the treatment have mostly relied
on aid from the US, for example.

It's true that some part of PEPFAR
was resumed, but it's not all.

And that means that the space
and the gap that has been left
needs to be filled.

And while that is happening,
people's lives are being impacted.

You mentioned PEPFAR.
That is the US's HIV program.

It stands for the US President's
Emergency Plan for Aids relief.

So there's PEPFAR,
there's USAID, and

it's all a bit confusing because they
were frozen, then unfrozen for a bit.

So now we are going to go to the US
to explain a bit more.

So earlier this year, President Trump
put a pause on all funding for USAID.

That's the United States Agency
for International Development.

And their job is to send money abroad
for things like health programmes,
especially in low-income countries,
and especially for things like HIV.

So when this 90-day freeze
took effect.

A lot of these programmes
took a hit because

they could no longer rely
on US support.
And one of these programmes was PEPFAR,

and it's been credited
with saving 26 million lives

with enabling 7.8 million babies
to be born without HIV.

So Congress went ahead
and stepped in.

They issued a waiver which meant
that they could access the funding
immediately.
But even by then, it was too late,

and a lot of these systems
were already dismantled.
So a lot of these clinics had closed.

A lot of these doctors
had been relieved of their duties.

And a lot of people
stopped taking their medication

because they didn't have access
to it any more.

So, Makuochi, are any of the countries on
the continent filling these funding
gaps themselves?

Well, the truth is,
the cut was a bit unprecedented and

I'm sure many countries didn't make
provision for it in their budgets.

But what has happened is
at the wake of this cut,

countries like Nigeria,
Malawi and Uganda

have sort of made quick adjustments
in their budget or made provisions

to help see that people living
with HIV are cared for and protected.

And even those, you know,
communities who might be

at risk are also given the care
that they need.

So these are sort of the quick
adjustments that countries have made.

but it will be interesting
to see how these roles

and if it can fill up the gap
already existing after this cut.

At the start of the episode,
I mentioned the UN's goal,

which is to completely get rid
of all new cases of HIV by 2030.

Are there any countries that are
on track to do that?

Well, countries like Uganda
had achieved over 97% coverage

for their target in reaching
both preventions and testing for HIV.

Even South Africa had
also done quite well in over 90%

or about 90% in their targets
towards reaching this coverage

in terms of both treatment
and prevention.

So yes, many
or some countries have been on track,

but with this sudden cut,
it's a bit worrisome how this will be

and what could play out,
because lots

of experts have raised their concerns
about how the impact is cutting,

this cut would have
in the progress being made.

Apart from the international funding
they were getting,

what kind of strategies
were they using to get?

You know, you mentioned
97... 90% elimination rates.

First of all, what some countries did
was to integrate their HIV treatment

and prevention and testing programmes
into the primary healthcare.

So that means it was easy to walk
into any primary healthcare

and get the care you need, and even
get your medication or get tested.

And mostly, and not only were these
available for most countries,

it became subsidised or completely
free, so you didn't have to think

of paying out of pocket to access
these programmes.

What other countries
also did was to move

their treatment
or their programmes to communities.

So you don't have to think
of going to the hospitals.

The programmes did come
into your community,

and that also provided an avenue
to ensure that there were

public health awareness
in those communities.

You know, teaching people
about prevention, teaching people

about what treatments are available,
teaching people about

what misinformation is accurate
or what is inaccurate.

So these are some of the programmes
or strategies
that some countries did implement.

So what do you think it would take
for us to reach that target globally,

to get rid
of all new cases of HIV by 2030?

It's tricky to say, but some of the
experts I've spoken to have told me

that things like increased funding
would totally help – increased funding

in HIV and Aids programmes –
but also even funding research

around finding new ways
to either combat HIV or reduce HIV.

As we know,
there are lots of research going on,

but, you know,
improving and advancing this research

could perhaps help
us come quite close to, you know,
stopping HIV by 2030.

But also there is there is also need
to make more efforts around stopping

or reducing stigma and misinformation
around HIV and Aids.

These are some of the things
that can help us get there by 2030.

Makuochi, thank you.
Thank you so much, Hannah.
And thank you for joining us.

This is What in the World
from the BBC World Service.

I'm Hannah Gelbart
and we'll see you next time. Bye.
